results.possibleIdentity: Bernard Kahn was a research engineer and physicist active during the 1960s and 1970s, primarily associated with the Curtiss-Wright Corporation in Wood-Ridge, New Jersey. He specialized in advanced technical fields including electrogasdynamics (EGD), magnetohydrodynamics (MHD), and thin-film electronic components. Kahn is notably recognized for his collaboration with the prominent physicist Meredith C. Gourdine; together, they co-authored the seminal paper "Electrogasdynamic power generation," published in the AIAA Journal in 1964. His work at Curtiss-Wright involved research into high-vacuum thin-film projects, radar systems, and power conversion technologies, such as pulse-width-modulated inverters. In the late 1960s, Kahn was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a collaborative movement that paired engineers with artists for innovative exhibitions, including one held between November 1968 and February 1969. During this period, he resided in Tenafly, New Jersey.
